explicit_text: I was born and raised in Japan, where I had eight years of education before coming to America at 19. My family lived in a farming village, and my father was a farmer who owned a little land but mostly leased it. Growing up, I enjoyed school, especially math and calligraphy, and I was one of the few students who never missed a day. Although my family followed the Zen sect of Buddhism, they were not very devout.
implicit_text: I was born and raised in an East Asian island nation, where I had eight years of education before coming to America at 19. My family lived in a farming village, and my father was a farmer who owned a little land but mostly leased it. Growing up, I enjoyed school, especially math and calligraphy, and I was one of the few students who never missed a day. Although my family followed a particular sect of Buddhism, they were not very devout.

explicit_text: My family practiced the Zen sect of Buddhism back in Japan, but we were not very devout. It was the religion of my household, though I don't recall them being deeply religious. The Zen beliefs were part of my upbringing, but I eventually moved away from that faith after coming to America.

explicit_text: I arrived in Seattle when I was 19, after jumping off a ship tied at the pier. I stayed at a hotel there for a couple of days before heading to Montana to work on the railroad. Later, in the summer of 1920, I returned to Seattle to work and attend night school, and eventually I worked as an auto mechanic apprentice and lived at the Buddhist Church. Seattle was where I learned English and became involved with the Japanese and Chinese Mission and the Japanese Congregational Church.

I stayed at a hotel there for a couple of days before heading to Montana to work on the railroad. Later, in the summer of 1920, I returned to that city to work and attend night school, and eventually I worked as an auto mechanic apprentice and lived at the Buddhist Church. That city was where I learned English and became involved with the Japanese and Chinese Mission and the Japanese Congregational Church.

explicit_text: After arriving in America, I went to Montana to work on the railroad for about a year. I worked mainly cleaning ashes from the engines on the Great Northern and Northern Pacific railroads. We stayed in Troy, which was located between Whitefish and Spokane. It was hard work but I saved around $800 before moving back to Seattle.

explicit_text: In Montana, I worked on the Great Northern Railway cleaning ashes from the engines and turning them around in the roundhouse. This was tough physical labor, but I also became an oil boy after learning some English. The Great Northern was one of the two railroads I worked for during that time.

explicit_text: Alongside the Great Northern, I worked for the Northern Pacific Railway in Montana. My job involved cleaning ashes and extinguishing fires on the engines when trains arrived from Spokane and Whitefish. It was hard but necessary work, and I learned a lot about the railroad operations during that year.

explicit_text: While working on the railroad in Montana, I stayed in Troy, which was located between Whitefish and Spokane. From there, we serviced trains coming from both towns, cleaning ashes and turning engines around in the roundhouse. Troy was my base during that year of hard railroad labor.

explicit_text: Whitefish was one of the towns connected by the railroad where I worked in Montana. Trains would come from Whitefish to Troy, where we cleaned the engines and turned them around. It was part of the route I serviced during my year on the Great Northern and Northern Pacific railroads.

explicit_text: Spokane was another key town on the railroad route where I worked in Montana. Trains from Spokane arrived at Troy, and we cleaned the ashes and extinguished fires on the engines before sending them back. Spokane was part of the circuit that kept the trains running smoothly.

explicit_text: I learned English gradually, starting on the ship where English was used for tools and navigation. Later, in Seattle, I attended night school and the Japanese and Chinese Mission where English was taught for free. Learning English helped me become an oil boy on the railroad and later allowed me to work as a chauffeur for white families to improve my conversation skills.

explicit_text: I heard about Vancouver in relation to immigration and passports. The hotel owner who helped me mentioned that he did not have a passport from Vancouver but came to Seattle with the help of an immigration officer. Vancouver was part of the immigration context during my early days in America.

explicit_text: In Seattle, I attended the Japanese and Chinese Mission located in the basement of the First Presbyterian Church. They offered free English classes and Bible study mainly focused on the Old Testament. The mission was important for my spiritual growth and learning, and I attended regularly on Sundays and Thursdays.

They taught us the Old Testament and hymns and helped nurture the immigrant Christian community. It was through their night school that I deepened my understanding of Christianity before joining the Japanese Congregational Church.

explicit_text: I worked as an unpaid apprentice mechanic at the Ikeda brothers' used car business in Seattle. The older Mr. Ikeda was the elder of the Japanese Congregational Church, and his younger brother was a deacon. After starting work there, I began attending the Congregational Church and was baptized by Rev. Abe in December 1920.

explicit_text: When I first came to Seattle, I stayed at the Buddhist Church for $6 a month, which included breakfast and supper. They provided clean sheets once a week, and I changed my own sheets. Although I lived there initially, I eventually left the Buddhist Church after becoming a Christian and joining the Japanese Congregational Church.

explicit_text: After starting work with the Ikeda brothers, I began attending the Japanese Congregational Church in Seattle. They held three services on Sunday, including morning worship, Christian Endeavor, and evangelical meetings. I was baptized there by Rev. Abe on December 19, 1920, and it became my spiritual home.

explicit_text: My wife was learning etiquette in Oregon when we were introduced by her aunt, who also brought her to the church. Later, when her father became ill in Oregon, I quit my chauffeur job to help him with farming. Oregon was significant in our family life and responsibilities after marriage.

explicit_text: One of the white families I worked for as a chauffeur in Seattle was Jewish, and the man sold Warner Brothers pictures in the Seattle area. He liked Japanese people and paid me well compared to other families. This job helped me improve my English and earn a better salary.

explicit_text: The Jenkins family, for whom I worked as a chauffeur, lived in a summer house at Three Tree Point between Seattle and Tacoma. I drove Mrs. Jenkins to Seattle every day while Mr. Jenkins drove his own car. This location was part of my work life after marriage.
